title: Cirrus: Print images that should be pruned
body:
Over time unless they're removed, the project could grow quite a large
collection of VM images.  While generally cheap (less than a penny each,
per month), these will become a significant cost item if not kept
in-check.

Add a specialized container for handling image-pruning, but limit
it to only finding and printing (not actually deleting) images.

Also update the image-building workflow so that base-images used to
compose cache-images are also labeled with metadata.

N/B: As an additional safeguard, the service account which
     executes the new container in production *DOES NOT*
     have access to delete images.  This can be enabled
     by adding the GCE IAM role: CustomComputeImagePrune

Signed-off-by: Chris Evich <cevich@redhat.com>

comments.body: ***NOTE:*** This is completely untested as of this comment.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #3308) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.

@edsantiago mind taking an initial look?  I know you're not fond of the `entrypoint.sh` name, but I kept it for consistency with other images.  This new task and container image is the counter-part of time-stamping the "last used" datestamp on VM images (meta task). However, the new one only runs post-merge, on the master branch, and (for now) only prints the victim's names.  [Example run of new task in Cirrus-CI using hand-built image](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5713695240355840)

> I need to make another pass but I'm drained right now.

No worries, my preference is to avoid accidents here :smile: 

I'm conflicted about whether or not to have image_prune `depends_on` (in the yaml) include some of the testing tasks.  On one hand, it allows useful testing to happen _before_ an accidental in-use image deletion.  On the other hand, it means we find out about the accident indirectly, by a future build failing (due to missing image).  I'm leaning toward it being best to keep the test-failure as near as possible to the prune-accident (i.e. in the same job).

---

There's also an obscure systematic failure mode caused by the 'meta' task NOT erroring if updating 'lastUsed' breaks.  The team decided a while ago it wasn't worth my time/effort to maintain separate image-build/test loops for for every project.  So they all share libpod's project space and use the 'imgts' container to update metadata.

So, consider if metadata updates work fine for a while, but then start failing and go unnoticed on another project.  That means  image-pruning (in this project) could kill images being used elsewhere.  I can't see a way to fix that which doesn't involve lots of work here or in the other projects.  Though perhaps this is simply unlikely to happen.  Also the consequence isn't too horrible, since producing replacement images is fully scripted.

I considered this: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/images/deprecate but note all the `purely informational` mentions.  I don't like the idea of adding yet-another processing loop to handle deprecated/deleted states, too :confounded: Complicated.

Should I make metadata update failures by imgts fatal, and document why it's such a potentially horrible thing if/when it ever fails?

rebased and force-pushed.  Only change was a minor README fix.

Ignoring the big scary image delete problem for now.
Changes LGTM, tests aren't hip and it looks the same as your other PR's issues.
